steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures around the blank
steps.content: Before the blank, you have a dependent clause: “when searching for food.” After the blank, starting with “in the Northern Hemisphere,” you have an independent clause: “the magnetic field points down, into the ground, but in the Southern Hemisphere, it points up...” The task is to link a dependent clause to an independent clause that explains the first idea.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule for explanation
steps.content:
A colon introduces an explanation or elaboration. Here, the magnetic field information explains why the worms move opposite to the field when searching for food—because field orientation differs between hemispheres.

Using a colon after “food:” makes this causal link explicit.

B (comma) creates a comma splice: a comma alone cannot separate an introductory phrase (“in the Northern Hemisphere”) from the independent clause (“the magnetic field points down”).

C (“while”) is grammatically possible but awkward and redundant since the temporal relationship is already clear from “when.” D (no punctuation) leaves the relationship between the two ideas ambiguous.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
